coredata.dc:title: Temperature impact on the morphological evolution of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-decorated zinc oxide and its influence on highly efficient visible-light photocatalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Widiyandari, Hendri
Al Ja'farawy, Muhammad Shalahuddin
Parasdila, Hanaiyah
Astuti, Yayuk
Arutanti, Osi
Mufti, Nandang
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-decorated zinc oxide (ZnO/NCQD) was synthesized using a hydrothermal process under various thermal conditions. XRD (X-Ray Diffractometer) and field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) confirmed the crystal structure of wurtzite, and there was a morphological evolution from a hexagonal nanorod to a nanoflower. Simultaneously, the optical properties and photocatalytic activity were investigated using UV–visible diffuse reflectance, photoluminescence, and UV–vis spectrophotometers. The presence of NCQD on the ZnO surface lowers the bandgap energy and shifts the absorbance toward visible light. As a result, it enhanced the photocatalytic activity and efficiency of ZnO/NCQD, achieving approximately 80% methylene blue (MB) degradation in 15 min under visible light. It was concluded that the hydrothermal temperature played a role in the particle growth of the ZnO/NCQD and affected the optical and photocatalytic properties. Therefore, the fabricated ZnO/NCQD has excellent potential for photocatalytic dye degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalyst
Morphological evolution
ZnO
Carbon quantum dots
Hydrothermal
Photodegradation

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education Culture Research and Technology

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology Republic of Indonesia

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology Republic of Indonesia under the grant of Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia with contract number: 590.1/UN27.22 / HK.07.00/2023. The author (HW) thanks the Center of Excellence for Electrical Energy Storage Technology, Universitas Sebelas Maret, for facilitating FTIR and UV–vis spectroscopy characterization.

This research was funded by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology Republic of Indonesia under the grant of Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia with contract number: 590.1/UN27.22/ HK.07.00/2023. The author (HW) thanks the Center of Excellence for Electrical Energy Storage Technology, Universitas Sebelas Maret, for facilitating FTIR and UV–vis spectroscopy characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Diffraction peaks of ZnO/NCQD in different hydrothermal temperatures; (b) shifted diffraction peaks of ZnO/NCQD.
Optical image of NCQD: (a) under natural daylight and (b) UV light exposure (365 nm) and (c) water under UV light exposure (365 nm).
(a) Hexagonal unit cell structure; (b) Illustration of the flower-like ZnO growth [30].

FE-SEM images of (a) ZnO, (b) ZnO/NCQD 100, (c) ZnO/NCQD 115, (d) ZnO/NCQD 130, (e) ZnO/NCQD 145, and (f) ZnO/NCQD 160; TEM images of (g) ZnO/NCQD; (h) NCQD; (j) magnification of NCQD on the surface of ZnO; (j–l) element mapping of ZnO/NCQD.
FTIR spectra of (a) NCQD, (b) ZnO standard, ZnO nanoflower, and ZnO/NCQD.
(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms, and (b) dV/dD pore volume vs pore diameter of ZnO/NCQD.
(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of ZnO and ZnO/NCQD; (b) Band gap energy of ZnO/NCQD with different hydrothermal temperatures.

(a) PL spectra of ZnO/NCQD with different hydrothermal temperatures (
λ
e x c
= 395 nm); (b) Magnification of PL spectra of ZnO/CQD.
UV–visible absorption spectra for MB degradation on (a) ZnO, (b) ZnO/NCQD 100, (c) ZnO/NCQD 115, (d) ZnO/NCQD 130, (e) ZnO/NCQD 145, and (f) ZnO/NCQD 160.
(a) Degradation percentage of MB solution during the photodegradation of ZnO/NCQD; (b) Reaction rate kinetics of ZnO/NCQD.
Texture coefficient (TC) value of ZnO/NCQD with various hydrothermal temperature.
Mass fraction and atomic mass percentage of ZnO/NCQD determined by EDX spectra.
BET analysis surface area and porosity of ZnO nanoflower and ZnO/NCQD.
Kinetic equation and parameters for the degradation of MB solution with different photocatalyst.

head.ce:title: Temperature impact on the morphological evolution of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-decorated zinc oxide and its influence on highly efficient visible-light photocatalyst

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Mathematics and Natural Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-decorated zinc oxide (ZnO/NCQD) was synthesized using a hydrothermal process under various thermal conditions. XRD (X-Ray Diffractometer) and field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) confirmed the crystal structure of wurtzite, and there was a morphological evolution from a hexagonal nanorod to a nanoflower. Simultaneously, the optical properties and photocatalytic activity were investigated using UV–visible diffuse reflectance, photoluminescence, and UV–vis spectrophotometers. The presence of NCQD on the ZnO surface lowers the bandgap energy and shifts the absorbance toward visible light. As a result, it enhanced the photocatalytic activity and efficiency of ZnO/NCQD, achieving approximately 80% methylene blue (MB) degradation in 15 min under visible light. It was concluded that the hydrothermal temperature played a role in the particle growth of the ZnO/NCQD and affected the optical and photocatalytic properties. Therefore, the fabricated ZnO/NCQD has excellent potential for photocatalytic dye degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, although organic pollutants in water have decreased, the textile industry and other chemical industries have grown to be significant pollutants in the water environment. Semiconductor metal oxides with exceptional photocatalytic activity, such as Zn2TiO4, TiO2, ZnO, Mn2O3/WO3, Bismuth molybdates (BMO), and ZnS, are the most promising materials for the removal of organic pollutants [1–4]. TiO2 and ZnO, two semiconductor metal oxides with similar band gaps (ZnO, 3.37 eV and TiO2 (3.2 eV), are good photocatalysts for water treatment [5].

Owing to its additional benefits, including a high exciton binding energy (60 meV), high quantum efficiency, high photostability, oxidation resistance, and low manufacturing costs, ZnO is a possible substitute [6]. However, owing to its broad bandgap, it is difficult to use solar light as a reliable, abundant, and secure source of photoexcitation for practical photocatalytic applications. Over the past decade, numerous methods have been developed for the application of ZnO photocatalysts under visible light to overcome this obstacle [7].

To overcome the disadvantages of ZnO, nanostructures are important to achieve superior structural stability, low agglomeration, and high photocatalytic activity [8]. Several nanostructures have been developed, including nanowires, nanorods, nanosheets, nanobelts, nanotubes, nanoflowers, and nanospheres [9]. The flower-like morphology is a unique three-dimensional structure of nano-ZnO. This is because the flower-like structure exhibits good performance in photoluminescence and photocatalysis [10]. These characteristics are attributed to the large specific surface areas and their ability to prevent single nanomaterial aggregation [11].

In addition to changing the morphology of ZnO, many other techniques have been applied, such as metal or non-metal doping, precious metal deposition, heterojunction production, and carbon compound splicing [12]. Combining carbon materials with ZnO has recently become a major procedure because of its role as a photoelectron reservoir, which absorbs and transmits photogenerated electrons from ZnO to the substrate. The photosensitizing properties of ZnO enable it to absorb light in the visible and near-infrared ranges [13]. In the study of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot-decorated zinc oxide (ZnO/NCQD) and other carbon materials, the ability of NCQD in wastewater treatment has gained attention because of its high stability, low toxicity, high water dispersibility, and good photostability [14]. The quantum confinement effect, oxygenated functional groups on the NCQD surface, and electron transit and reservoir properties contribute to the advantages of ZnO/NCQD's for segregating photogenerated electrons [15].

Recently, researchers have contributed to the development of NCQDs, such as their synthesis methods, optical properties, and application prospects. To generate NCQD with stable optical properties and excellent catalytic performance, doping NCQDs with some nonmetallic elements (such as N, P, S, and Cl) is an effective strategy. Several reports have shown a positive result for NCQDs doped with nonmetallic elements, and Sharma et al. [16] reported N-doped ZnO/C-dots nanoflower, which exhibited higher photocatalytic activity than N–ZnO and ZnO commercial. Behnood and Sodeifian [17] reported the preparation of N-CQDs/Ni–ZnO, in which the amount of N-CQDs had a significant impact on the photocatalytic activity.

Temperature, solution concentration, and solvent pH affect the formation of ZnO nanoparticles, which can adopt various morphologies [18]. The hydrothermal growth of ZnO nanostructures is the most widely explored method for controlling the growth of ZnO via the reaction temperature, time, and molar ratio, which results in different properties of ZnO nanoflowers [19]. Sahu et al. [20] reported that the hydrothermal temperature plays a vital role in forming different patterns of flowers and enhancing crystallinity with increasing temperature. However, the doping NCQD onto the ZnO surface require perfect conditions, such as the morphology and particle size of ZnO. In this study, we investigated the impact of a perfect hydrothermal temperature on ZnO growth, which has a flower-like morphological evolution to combine with NCQD and it will be applied to the photodegradation process to examine the photocatalytic activity reaction produced by the sample.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2
∙
6H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, Germany), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Indonesia), and trisodium citrate dihydrate (C6H5Na3O7
∙
2H2O, Merck, Germany) were used to create zinc-oxide-like flowers. Citric acid (C6H8O7; PT. Brataco, Indonesia) and urea (CO(NH2)2, Merck, Germany) were used in the preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots. All chemicals were used without prior purification, and the samples were washed with distilled water and ethanol (C2H5OH, Merck, Germany).

Widiyandari et al. [21] used 3.72 g Zn(NO3)2
∙
6H2O and 1.47 g C6H5Na3O7
∙
2H2O in distilled water (10 mL) to create a flower-like ZnO synthesis method. The NaOH 1 M solution was also prepared simultaneously, and it was drip-added to the mixture while stirring until the pH reached 13. The mixture was rapidly stirred for 100 min at 27 °C (room temperature) before being placed in an autoclave for the hydrothermal process. The hydrothermal process involved treating the material in a furnace for 10 h at temperatures of 100, 115, 130, 145, and 160 °C. The fabricated products were then dried with filter paper and washed with distilled water and ethanol. After washing, the residue was dried at 100 °C for 4 h, followed by 3 h of calcination at 500 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: Using distilled water and under vigorous stirring, 2 mmol C6H8O7 and 6 mmol CO(NH2)2 were dissolved in 25 mL nitrogen for 10 min to prepare NCQDs solution. Subsequently, the produced solution was put into an autoclave for 8 h at 160 °C to initiate the hydrothermal process.

To synthesize ZnO/NCQD, 2 g of ZnO was dissolved in 45 mL of distilled water while stirring, and then 1.5 mL of the NCQD solution was added and stirred the solution continuously for 2 h. The resulting mixture was then put into the autoclave to initiate the hydrothermal process for 8 h at 100 °C. Using a filter paper, the finished product was dried before being cleaned with distilled water and ethanol, and then dried at 100 °C for 4 h.

Field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM JIB-4610 F) to determine the morphology of the samples, The Fourier Transmission Infra-Red (FTIR, Shimadzu IR Spirit) was performed to examine the functional group of the final product, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2 20 S-Twin), and X-ray diffraction (Rigaku SmartLab) were used to examine the surface morphology and structural characteristics of the prepared samples under Cu-Kα at room temperature in the 2° range of 17°–71°. Based on XRD data obtained, we could use them to calculate some following parameters, which are: The TC parameters were calculated according Eq. (1) , where
I
(
h i
k i
l i
)
is the intensity of the (
h i
k i
l i
) diffraction peak of the sample under investigation,
I
o
(
h i
k i
l i
)
is the intensity of the (
h i
k i
l i
) plane on the powder diffraction file card, and
N
is the number of diffractions considered in the analysis of XRD [22].
(1)
T
C

h i

l i
)

I

The average crystallite size will calculate using Debye Scherrer's law, as given in Eq. (2) [20].

k
= 0.89),

is the X-ray wavelength,

is the FWHM, and

To analyze the lattice parameters, the dislocation densities of the samples will calculate using Eq. (4) , where

is the dislocation density and

1
D 2

To obtain the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) data, the surface area of the ZnO/NCQD samples and N2 adsorption-desorption measurements were performed at 77 K on a Micromeritics Tristar II Plus 3020 instruments. The optical characteristics of the produced samples were assessed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Lambda 25), UV–Vis diffuse reflectance (MAYA Pro2000 Ocean Optics) to obtain the bandgap energy data, the bandgap energy of the ZnO/NCQDs was calculated according to Eq. (5) , where the value of
n = 1 / 2
indicates a direct band gap [20]. And photoluminescence (PerkinElmer LS 55).
(5)
(
α h υ
)
1 / n
= A
(
h υ −
E g
)

ZnO/NCQD samples were prepared for the photodegradation of methylene blue (MB) dyes in aqueous media under visible-light irradiation using a light simulator (solar simulator, Peccel/PEC-L01) to investigate their photocatalytic activity. The distance of the lamp to the sample was adjusted to 11 cm. To perform the photocatalytic reaction, 50 mL of the MB solution (10 ppm) was mixed with 100 mg of ZnO/NCQD photocatalyst. The suspension was then agitated in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the dye molecules and photocatalyst particles. The suspension was then exposed to visible-light irradiation while pipetting every 15 min. The photocatalyst material was exposed to visible light before centrifugation for 30 min at 6000 rpm to remove it from the dye solution. Utilizing the absorbance information from a UV–Vis spectrometer, the concentration of the dye solution could be determined. The photocatalytic activity of ZnO/NCQDs will investigate using Eq. (6) [36], where
C 0
and
C t
are the initial MB concentration and absorbance after t min of irradiation,
(6)
D e g r a d a t i o n
% =
C 0
−
C t
C 0
× 100 %
And the further investigation, the kinetic degradation of the MB solution under visible light, according to Behnood et al. [34], follows a first-order reaction calculated by the Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L-H) model, as shown in Eq. (7) .
(7)
− ln
(
C /
C 0
)
= k t

ZnO/NCQD was analyzed through XRD, the results revealed a hexagonal structure with all the diffraction peaks matching the first three crystal planes (100), (002), and (101), which are associated with the ZnO structure shown in Fig. 1 (a). This pattern is consistent with the ZnO JCPDS data (reference number 36–1451). The modest amount (10%), high dispersion, and low crystallinity of the N-CQDs prevented any diffraction peaks from being observed [24]. Fig. 1(a) illustrates how the number of NCQD on the ZnO surface affects the intensity of the diffraction peaks. The X-rays absorbed by the NCQD on the ZnO surface could reduce the intensity [23]. Because of the interaction between ZnO and NCQD, which is influenced by the lattice constant of ZnO, the presence of NCQD causes the diffraction peaks to shift (Fig. 1(b)) [24]. This illustrates how the crystal structure is affected by the intense physical contact between NCQD and ZnO.

Further analysis was conducted to investigate the growth orientation of ZnO/NCQDs at various hydrothermal temperatures by calculating the texture coefficient (TC). Based on the TC parameter values in Table 1 , it is indicated that the growth orientation of ZnO/NCQD is preferred on the
( 101 )
crystalline plane because the TC parameter value is higher than 1. According to Wang et al. [22], a greater value of TC (>1) indicates that the flower petal has grown along the c-axis as a result of the equilibrium between both the kinetic and thermodynamic processes involved in surface development. The hydrothermal temperature also caused discernible alterations in the (101) crystalline plane.

The lattice strain exhibited a negative trend as the hydrothermal temperature increased. It has been proven that the relationship between strain and crystal size is inverse. Table 2 summarizes the results of the calculations using Eq. (3) [20]. The lattice strain values obtained for ZnO/NCQD 100, ZnO/NCQD 115, ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160 were 0.020, 0.010, 0.012, 0.013, and 0.011, respectively. However, the ZnO/NCQD 115 sample, which was heated to 115 °C, had the lowest lattice strain value of all the samples, as well as the absence of crystallinity. According to Lupan et al. [26], increasing the temperature can release the intrinsic strain of the sample. Thus, the lattice strain values decreased with increasing hydrothermal temperature.

Apart from the presence of NCQD, the increase in hydrothermal temperature caused the peaks to shift owing to changes in the lattice parameter. The lattice parameter of ZnO/NCQD also decreased from that of the ZnO reference (JCPDS 36–1451:
a
= 3.249;
c / a
= 1.602). The decreasing lattice parameter might be caused by impurities (NCQD on the surface of ZnO), the change in shape from hexagonal to flower structure, and the particle size that is related to electrostatic forces [27]. The
c / a
value obtained was close to the reference value of 1.602, which indicates that all samples were in good hexagonality because the values of
c / a
indicate the ideal hexagonality for hexagonal close-packed (hcp) [28].

Intrinsic strain is a measure of lattice parameters arising from different factors. Dislocation density is a major factor related to strain. To analyze the lattice parameters, the dislocation densities of the samples were calculated using Eq. (4) [23]. Table 2 shows the decrease in dislocation density at higher hydrothermal temperatures caused by the increasing crystallite size and decreasing lattice strain. The ZnO/NCQD 100, ZnO/NCQD 115, ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160 sample calculated values are 3.39 nm−2, 0.81 nm−2, 1.34 nm−2, 1.46 nm−2, and 1.14 nm−2, respectively. Based on Sahu and Kar [20], crystals with larger dislocation densities are harder, and reducing the dislocation density improves the photocatalytic properties of ZnO/NCQDs.

Fig. 2 shows the physical characteristics of the NCQD-dispersed solution when exposed to visible light and UV radiation (365 nm). As shown in the figure, UV light irradiation causes NCQD to glow visibly in a distinctive manner. This is because NCQDs can generate strong blue fluorescence when exposed to UV light owing to their small grain size and variable surface state of the emission trap on the surface of the carbon nanoparticles, a phenomenon known as the quantum confinement size effect.

The ZnO/NCQD morphology at various hydrothermal temperatures was revealed using FE-SEM. Under various thermal conditions, the ZnO/morphology NCQD's showed a small difference, indicating the morphological evolution of the samples. The development of petals with stem-like shapes marks the beginning of the flower structure. The Coulomb attraction (0001) of the polar surface causes the petals to come together, creating a flower.

Temperature, Zn2+ and OH− contents, and floral growth play significant roles. According to Sahu and Kar [20], the initial homogeneous nucleation and supersaturated ZnO nuclei assembled because of the high reactant concentration. The initial nucleation of ZnO is affected by the low concentration of the ZnO2 2− monomer. Conversely, positively charged Zn absorbs a large amount of OH− from the solution (0001). Therefore, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a), a pH solution of 13 led the rods to develop in the direction of the nucleus (0001) and created a flower structure [29]. This is related to superfluous OH− ion, which stabilizes the surface charge and the structure of the
( 0001 )
surfaces. During the reaction process, zinc nitrate hexahydrate produces Zn2+ cations, which readily react with OH− anions to form [Zn(OH)4]2- as a growth unit. Thereafter, [Zn(OH)4]2- ions are decomposed to generate ZnO via the following reaction [30]:
Z
n
2 +
+ O
H −
→
[
Z n
(
O H
)
4
]
2 −
[
Z n
(
O H
)
4
]
2 −
→ Z n O +
H 2
+ 2 O
H −

Fig. 3 (b) illustrates the formation of ZnO/NCQD-like flowers with ZnO nuclei growing along
[
01
1 ‾
0
]
and leading to the formation of ZnO sheets with
{
2
1 ‾
1 ‾
0
}
planar surfaces. The ZnO rods were then gathered and assembled at one point to reduce the surface energy and form the center of the flower. The different hydrothermal temperatures caused different sizes and shapes of the flowers owing to the van der Wall force [20], as shown in Fig. 3. Ultra-small carbon quantum dots treated by a hydrothermal process can be easily assembled on petals of flower-like ZnO [31].

Several morphologies of the ZnO/NCQDs at various hydrothermal temperatures were investigated, as shown in Fig. 4 . It can be observed that ZnO/NCQD shows a different morphology at different temperatures. The ZnO particles (Fig. 4 a), and ZnO/NCQD at lower temperatures of 100 and 115 °C, as shown in Fig. 4 (b–c), are still in the hexagonal nanorod phase and are not assembled in one place. The ZnO rods started assembling at 130 °C and grew along the
( 0001 )
direction. These non-uniform flower particles assembled and combine with other particles to form interlinked clusters and become larger flowers, as shown in Fig. 4 (d–f). In addition, the grain size of ZnO is inhomogeneous and uneven because there are spaces that cause the formation of central-radial pore structures between other flowers. The flower-like ZnO was also indiscernible because the distribution of the NCQD particles covered the surface of the flower-like ZnO.

The TEM image in Fig. 4 (g – i) confirms the presence of NCQD on the surface of ZnO, with size belong to 8–12 nm. The TEM images in Fig. 4(i) show that the interplanar NCQD were embedded into the ZnO nanoflower according to the interplanar spacings of 0.55 nm for NCQDs and the surrounding spacing of 0.24 nm for ZnO (based on Table 2).

The average particle size of ZnO/NCQD decreased with increasing hydrothermal temperature. The particle size of ZnO/NCQD 100, ZnO/NCQD 115, ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160 are 892.67, 638.63, 596.36, 478.05, and 402.3 nm, respectively. The length of rods petal forming in ZnO/NCQD also decreases gradually with increasing hydrothermal temperature, and the length petals of ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160 are 237.9, 170.5, and 139.7 nm, respectively. Lu and Yeh [32], obtained a similar trend, in which the particle size and production yield decreased with increasing temperature.

Partial dissolution might have caused this at high pressures and temperatures. The high temperature also causes the rod petals to become more mobile and results in dislocation and piling up, which reduces the strain energy of the system. The rearrangement of the rod petal dislocation into low-angle-tilt boundaries triggers the generation of subgrains. Furthermore, the high-temperature condition causes the atoms to vibrate in the lattice position and exchange energy with other atoms. When the atoms have sufficient energy, they diffuse to achieve the lowest strain energy and form small grains [33].

The elemental distribution mapping in Fig. 4 (j – l) and the energy despersive x-ray (EDX) spectra in Table 3 also confirm the presence of ZnO and NCQD in terms of mass fraction and atomic mass percentage. The element mapping in Fig. 4 (j – l) reveals the uniform distribution of Zn, O, and C, which indicates the uniform distribution of NCQD across the surface of ZnO. The results confirmed that the average ZnO/NCQD composite was composed of Zn (62.2%), O (17.2%), and C (12.3%). The presence of nitrogen on the CQD was investigated through fourier transform infrared (FTIR). Fig. 5 (a) shows the absorption band of NCQD, whose characteristic absorptions at 3054 cm−1 and 1457 cm−1 are clearly attributed to the CC and C–N stretching vibrations, whereas the broad absorption at 3054 cm−1 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration. The ZnO/NCQD sample in Fig. 5 (b) shows absorptions of 1623 cm−1 and 1432 cm−1, which represent the CC and C–N stretching vibrations. This indicated the presence of NCQD on the ZnO surface. Table 3 shows that ZNO/NCQD 130 contains a higher amount of NCQD than the other ZnO/NCQDs. This is related to the effective particle size of the flower-like ZnO incorporated with carbon quantum dot. Higher hydrothermal temperatures reduce the particle size. However, this reduces the probability of carbon quantum dots being incorporated into the flower-like ZnO particles.

The specific surface area and N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms data for the ZnO/NCQD samples are provided in Table 4 and Fig. 6 (a)-(b) were determined using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) test. Fig. 6 (a) showed all ZnO/NCQD samples’ N2 gas adsorption/desorption isotherms. Since the samples absorb N2 gas, the hysteresis patterns indicated that the materials is taking place in mesopores materials and belonging to the type IV by hysteresis loop. Along with Fig. 6 (b), pores diameter of all ZnO/NCQD samples are in the range of 2–50 nm, it also confirms the categorization of all ZnO/NCQD samples as mesopores materials. Considering the BET results of ZnO/NCQD (Table 4), it can be seen that the values for specific surface area are in the range 2.0117–14.1515 m2 g-1 and the largest specific surface area is obtained at the ZnO/NCQD 130 sample. When the temperature increases, the surface area decreases significantly. It is obvious that temperature of the hydrothermal process of ZnO/NCQD leads to a significant increase in specific surface area, but also a significant decrease in the volume and diameter of pores.

To investigate the function of the supported NCQD on the surface of flower-like ZnO under different hydrothermal thermal conditions, UV–Visible diffuse reflectance spectrocopy (DRS) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were comprehensively studied. Based on the results of DRS UV–Vis in Fig. 7 (a), it can be observed that all ZnO/NCQDs show similar absorption characteristics, with strong peaks of 320–390 nm, indicating strong absorption in the UV light region. The ZnO/NCQDs also showed weak absorption in the visible region (400–800 nm). It can be observed that ZnO supported with NCQD shows a slight red-shift, and the wavelength of the absorption edge increases.

The different hydrothermal temperatures of ZnO/NCQD affect the absorption intensity in the UV and visible regions. The order of UV absorption follows ZnO/NCQD 130 > ZnO Commercial > ZnO/NCQD 150 > ZnO/NCQD 100 > ZnO/NCQD 145 > ZnO/NCQD 115. The order of intensity in visible light was ZnO/NCQD 130 > ZnO/NCQD 160 > ZnO/NCQD 100 > ZnO/NCQD 145 > ZnO/NCQD 115 > ZnO. It has been proven that the presence of NCQD can increase the absorption of light in the visible region. ZnO/NCQD 130 shows a better absorption performance than the other samples owing to its combination with NCQD. As a result, a higher amount of CQD on the flower-like ZnO increases the absorption intensity in the UV and visible regions. The absorption spectra of ZnO/NCQD correspond to the NCQD content in EDX. Increasing the temperature in the preparation of flower-like ZnO results in flower-like ZnO particles of different sizes.

To adhere to NCQD, the flower-like ZnO must have an ideal particle size. However, higher NCQD levels may lead to aggregation on the ZnO surface, which reduces the optical characteristics [34]. In contrast, ZnO exhibits significant UV absorption and modest visible absorption because of the energy of its band gap, which absorbs more photons in the UV region and stimulates electrons from the valence band of ZnO to its conduction band. The small size of NCQD, which permits the energy band to expand and causes the conduction band to increase, creates an appropriate energy band between ZnO and NCQD [35].

Fig. 7 (b) shows the Tauc plots for the optical bandgap determination of ZnO/NCQD. The bandgap energy of the ZnO/NCQDs was calculated according to Eq. (7) [20]. It can be observed that the band gap energies of ZnO/NCQD 100, ZnO/NCQD 115, ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160 are 3.170, 3.211, 3.124, 3.170, and 3.152 eV. The bandgap energy corresponds to the absorbance spectra, where ZnO/NCQD 130 shows a narrower bandgap than the other samples. More photogenerated electron-hole pairs are produced in the photocatalytic reaction owing to the narrow band gap and higher visible light absorption [25]. According to Efa and Imae [36], the bandgap narrows as the particle size decreases. However, the ideal size for NCQD integration exhibits a lower bandgap because of a decrease in the conductive-band level. This is the reason for the difference in the band gaps of ZnO/NCQD 130 and ZnO/NCQD 160.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained at an excitation of 395 nm to examine the photogenerated charge separation and recombination behaviors of ZnO/NCQD under various temperature conditions, as shown in Fig. 8 (a). Identical curve morphologies can be observed in the PL spectra of all samples, indicating that the presence of NCQD on the surface of flower-like ZnO does not provide a new PL signal. The PL spectra of ZnO/NCQD showed strong emissions at 421 nm and 447 nm, with multiple peaks at 485 nm, 527 nm, and 540 nm. The emission peaks at approximately 420 and 447 nm were possibly caused by interface radiation, surface oxygen vacancies, and defects in the flower-like ZnO [23]. The higher photogenerated electron-hole pair recombination rate is indicated by a higher PL emission intensity. Conversely, a lower PL emission intensity denotes quick charge transfer, electron capture at defect sites, and a decreased rate of recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, which might improve the photocatalytic activity [34]. As shown in Fig. 8 (b), the hydrothermal temperature affected the PL intensity of ZnO/NCQD, with ZnO/NCQD 145 < ZnO/NCQD 100 < ZnO/NCQD 130 < ZnO/NCQD 160 < ZnO/NCQD 115 < ZnO.

The photocatalytic performance of the ZnO/NCQDs was evaluated for the degradation of a MB solution under visible light irradiation. The MB solution was evaluated with and without the photocatalyst materials, and the MB was kept in the dark for 30 min to allow the photocatalyst material to absorb the MB molecule. As shown in Fig. 9 , the presence of the photocatalyst material degraded the MB solution, which is indicated by the decrease in the absorbance spectrum, and the appearance of the MB solution faded with increasing time.

The photocatalytic activity of ZnO/NCQDs was investigated using Eq. (6), where C 0 and C t are the initial MB concentration and absorbance after t min of irradiation, respectively. In Fig. 10 (a), the percentage degradation and concentration of MB were analyzed. The MB solution with the photocatalyst under dark conditions exhibited the lowest degradation compared to the other samples. This is because the electrons in the VB are not excited to the CB. The ZnO/NCQD 130 and ZnO/NCQD 160 exhibited superior photocatalytic performances. After 30 min, the MB solution was degraded by almost 80%, and then ZNO/NCQD 160 reached 93.2% at 75 min. This corresponds to the crystallinity, particle size, NCQD content, and band gap energy; increasing the hydrothermal temperature increases the crystallinity and decreases the particle size and band gap.

A smaller band gap energy of ZnO/NCQD 130 and ZnO/NCQD 160 makes it easier for electrons to excite under visible light irradiation and generate electron-hole pairs. The morphologies of the ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160, similar to that of the flower, resulted in better photocatalytic activity. Shende et al. [37] reported that nanoflower structures have a higher surface than other morphological structures because they consist of several petals. It was proven that the superior photocatalytic activities of ZnO/NCQD 130, ZnO/NCQD 145, and ZnO/NCQD 160, rather than ZnO/NCQD 100 and ZnO/NCQD 115, are still in the phase of the rod structure. The larger surface area provides more active sites for adsorption and interaction with the MB molecules.

According to Roza et al. [38], the electrical interaction between ZnO and NCQD improves the separation of photogenerated charges and enhances the transfer of e−/h+ couples. The increased electronic interaction of the carbon electrons with the conduction band of ZnO and subsequent up-conversion were produced by the work function of the NCQD, which is below the conduction band energy of ZnO [39]. Fig. 10 (b) shows the kinetic degradation of the MB solution under visible light, according to Behnood et al. [34], follows a first-order reaction calculated by the Langmuir–Hinshelwood (L-H) model (
Eq. (7)
.) The rate constant is summarized in Table 5 , we can see that the highest rate constant (
k
) is given by ZnO/NCQD 145 with 0.04358 min−1 and 0.92000 of R2.

Herein, flower-like ZnO/NCQD for MB degradation under visible light were successfully synthesized via the hydrothermal method under different temperature conditions. The different hydrothermal temperatures resulted in different crystallinities, morphologies, particle sizes, band gap energies, PL spectra, and photocatalytic activities. The effective hydrothermal temperature resulted in an optimum particle size for sticking out the NCQD. A higher amount of NCQD on the surface of ZnO decreases the band gap energy and shifts the absorbance to visible light. As a result, the photocatalytic activity of ZnO/NCQD was improved, with a higher efficiency reaching almost 80% MB degradation in 15 min.

Hendri Widiyandari: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Hanaiyah Parasdila: Writing – review & editing. Muhammad Shalahuddin Al Ja’farawy: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Osi Arutanti: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Yayuk Astuti: Writing – review & editing. Nandang Mufti: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Research, and Technology Republic of Indonesia under the grant of Riset Kolaborasi Indonesia with contract number: 590.1/UN27.22/ HK.07.00/2023. The author (HW) thanks the Center of Excellence for Electrical Energy Storage Technology, Universitas Sebelas Maret, for facilitating FTIR and UV–vis spectroscopy characterization.
